_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Claiborne County, MS. Formed in 1802 from Jefferson County. Port Gibson is the county seat — the town that General Grant reportedly declared 'too beautiful to burn' during the Vicksburg Campaign. Claiborne County sits on the Mississippi River and was part of the plantation belt, with a large enslaved population. The county's records survive well from the early 1800s.

current_courthouse.location: Port Gibson, MS — Main Street (rebuilt after 1900 fire on the same site)

previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced as the county grew. Records survived the transition.

previous_courthouses.location: Port Gibson — brick courthouse on Main Street
previous_courthouses.fate: A fire in 1900 damaged the courthouse structure but most bound record volumes were saved by citizens and officials who carried them from the building. Some loose papers and miscellaneous files were lost. The courthouse was rebuilt on the same site.

record_losses.description: A fire in 1900 damaged the Claiborne County courthouse in Port Gibson. The community responded by removing bound volumes from the building, saving the major record series. Loose papers — estate files, correspondence, and miscellaneous court documents — suffered greater losses. The courthouse was rebuilt in 1903. The fire's impact on records was significant but not catastrophic, thanks to the quick action of citizens.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} had loose estate papers, tax correspondence, or unbound court filings in the Claiborne County courthouse in 1900, those documents may have been lost in the fire. The bound deed books, wills, and marriage records survived — but the informal paper trail that often fills genealogical gaps did not.
record_losses.source: Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Guide to Official Records in the Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Jackson, 1994.

record_losses.cause: Battle of Port Gibson and Union occupation during Vicksburg Campaign
records_destroyed: some scattered papers during military occupation
records_survived: bound record volumes — Grant's army passed through but did not burn the town
record_losses.description: The Battle of Port Gibson was fought on May 1, 1863, as Grant's forces moved inland from their Mississippi River crossing at Bruinsburg. Confederate forces were driven from the field, and Port Gibson fell to Union troops. Grant is reported to have said the town was 'too beautiful to burn,' and it was not destroyed. The courthouse survived the battle and subsequent occupation. Some disruption of records occurred, but no systematic destruction.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Port Gibson survived the Vicksburg Campaign because Grant chose not to burn it. If {ancestor_name} was in Claiborne County in 1863, the Battle of Port Gibson was fought in {pronoun_possessive} front yard — but the records survived because a Union general admired the town.
record_losses.source: Bearss, Edwin C. The Campaign for Vicksburg. 3 vols. Dayton, OH: Morningside Press, 1985-1986.

deeds.completeness: Good — deed books survive from county formation. Some early books show fire damage from 1900 but remain legible.
deeds.gaps: No major gaps. Pre-1802 land transactions recorded in Jefferson County or Adams County parent records.
deeds.repository: Claiborne County Chancery Clerk, Port Gibson; Mississippi Department of Archives and History
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; limited MDAH digital collections

wills_estates.completeness: Good for bound volumes. Loose estate papers sustained losses in the 1900 fire.
wills_estates.gaps: Loose estate files from the earliest decades may be incomplete due to 1900 fire. Bound will books are intact.
wills_estates.repository: Claiborne County Chancery Clerk; Mississippi Department of Archives and History

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds and licenses survive from county formation.
marriages.gaps: Minimal gaps. Some early bonds show wear.
marriages.repository: Claiborne County Circuit Clerk; Mississippi Department of Archives and History

court_minutes.completeness: Good — bound court minute books survived both the 1863 battle and the 1900 fire.
court_minutes.gaps: Minor gap during Civil War military operations (1863).
court_minutes.repository: Claiborne County Circuit Clerk; Mississippi Department of Archives and History

vital_records.completeness: Mississippi statewide registration began 1912. Rural compliance was slow.
vital_records.repository: Mississippi State Department of Health, Vital Records

substitute_sources.source_type: Church records — Port Gibson Presbyterian and Baptist
substitute_sources.description: Port Gibson's churches — particularly the First Presbyterian Church (with its iconic hand-pointing-heavenward steeple) and Baptist congregations — maintained membership rolls, baptism records, and burial records. Black Baptist churches organized after emancipation also hold records.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual congregations; Mississippi Department of Archives and History; some records at Mississippi College archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Church records supplement civil records, particularly for marriages before Mississippi's 1912 vital registration law and for Black families whose civil record trail is thinner.
substitute_sources.source: Claiborne County Historical Society records; MDAH church records holdings.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1810-1940. Slave schedules (1850, 1860) document a county where enslaved people formed the majority of the population.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for all families. Slave schedules reflect Claiborne County's plantation economy.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Claiborne County, MS.

substitute_sources.description: Bureau records for Claiborne County document labor contracts, complaints, and marriage registrations for formerly enslaved families.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for tracing Black families from slavery through emancipation. Labor contracts often name former owners.
substitute_sources.source: Freedmen and Southern Society Project. Freedom: A Documentary History of Emancipation. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.

narrative_hooks: Port Gibson was 'too beautiful to burn' — or so Grant reportedly said — and because of that judgment, {ancestor_name}'s records survived a campaign that destroyed courthouses up and down the Mississippi. The town's beauty was its insurance policy.

The 1900 fire at the Claiborne County courthouse destroyed what the Civil War had not — the loose papers, the estate correspondence, the miscellaneous filings that might have filled the gaps in {ancestor_name}'s story. The bound books survived because people carried them out. The loose papers burned.

If {ancestor_name} was enslaved in Claiborne County, the estate inventories in the courthouse may record {pronoun_possessive} name and age — written in an appraiser's hand and denominated in dollars. The record exists because it was legally required to value property. That the property was a person is the fact the record refuses to acknowledge.

The Battle of Port Gibson was fought on May 1, 1863 — and if {ancestor_name} was in Claiborne County that day, the war was not a distant rumor. It was artillery fire audible from the courthouse steps.
source_references: Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Guide to Official Records in the Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Jackson, 1994.
Bearss, Edwin C. The Campaign for Vicksburg. 3 vols. Dayton, OH: Morningside Press, 1985-1986.
Davis, Ronald L. F. Good and Faithful Labor: From Slavery to Sharecropping in the Natchez District, 1860-1890. Westport, CT: Greenwood Press, 1982.
Claiborne County Historical Society records; Mississippi Department of Archives and History church records holdings.
